# Extracao: transcription_mp4_01-Bride_in_Need_virtual

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_01-Bride_in_Need_virtual.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.01 MB

---

Tim asks me if I have seen his fiancee and I remind him that Tina is actually his wife and not his fiancee. I understand the mistake. They've only been married for about four hours. From experience, I can tell you wife is a weird word to get used to. But I say it's your wife and it's going to be fine. It's not surprising that he asks me, although it should be. I'm not the best man. I'm not the father of the bride. I'm actually not even an invited guest at this wedding. I'm actually the DJ. I am just the guy playing mambo number five right now. If this was my first wedding or my 50th wedding or my 100th wedding, I would actually be surprised that he brought this problem to me. Because this is my 433rd wedding, I understand why Tim has brought this problem to me. Because wedding DJs, the music is not that important to be honest with you. It's not that hard to play the right music in the right way. The truth is wedding DJs are like the Swiss army knife of your wedding. We are the people who have seen it all. And so when a problem like I can't find my bride happens, we sort of understand what to do so that when you forget to seat your grandmother, because apparently you thought she would be dead by the time the wedding rolled around, I'm the guy who quickly makes a spot at table two for her so she doesn't even know that she's ever been forgotten. Or when your husband puts his fist all the way through the wedding cake and wipes chocolate all the way down your dress, I'm the one who plots revenge later on and then convinces you to get out of the car and to come back into the wedding and continue to celebrate even with your messy dress. Or when you agree to allow your uncle to play the accordion for one song during dancing, but he won't leave the dance floor. I am the person along with the best man who physically removes your uncle, not just from the dance floor, but from the entire facility. We throw him out on his ass. And when it's time for you to dance your first dance and you're a maid of honor and your mother can't figure out how to bustle the dress, I am the person who gets up in your business because I understand how to do the hooks. I know how to do the buttons. I can even do the tiny satin ties that are terrible, but I understand how to do them. So finding Tina is not going to be a problem. I say, go check the bathroom. It takes brides forever to pee, but the maid of honor is here and she says I've already looked. She's not there. And so I say, all right, well, there's a bridal suite in the building. She's probably, but no, she's not there either. The father of the bride is already there and Tina is not in the bridal suite. And I look around like it's hard to really lose a bride at a wedding. She's the only woman wearing a white dress unless you actually attended my wedding, at which point my sister also wore a white dress, which is something we don't talk about. But other than that one terrible situation, it's usually just the woman in the white dress and I don't see Tina. So I tell them not to worry. I tell my DJ partner, Benji, I say, you play mambo number six, I'll go find Tina. What happens typically is the bride is upset about something usually related to the mother-in-law and they go hide somewhere. And I've been in this facility a million times. So I know where they hide. I go into the kitchen first. It's the perfect place to find the bride, but she's not there. So I go to the rooms on the sides. I go to the coat closet where I once found a bride crying. I go to all the places I expect to find a bride and she's in none of these places. I'm kind of surprised. I'm getting a little worried. Like, where the hell is Tina? I eventually find myself on the front of the building coming out the entrance and all in front of me, it's a parking lot. And then on the other side, there's a gazebo near a pond. I think maybe she's gone there. Maybe she's just needed a minute. She knew the gazebo was there. So I'll go check over there. But I don't see her. Again, it's white. It's not, it's not a hard thing to see a bride's like they're not camouflaged in any way. So I start crossing the parking lot. And as I get closer and closer to the gazebo, I can see she's not there. She's not by the pond. And then I see her. I see her on the ground in the parking lot and she's leaned up against the tire of a truck in her wedding dress and she's crying. And so she sees me and recognizes me. She starts to get up and I say, no, no, no, stay right where you are. And I sit down on the parking lot next to her and I ask her what's going on. And she holds up her hand and in it, there's a cigarette. She tells me that she's a smoker and her husband, Tim, doesn't know it. And she had promised herself that on her wedding night, this would be the night she decided to quit smoking because they would move in together and she wouldn't be able to hide it anymore. And now on her wedding day, she has realized you don't get to quit smoking just by deciding the day to quit smoking. And she doesn't know what to do. And so I tell her what I tell all brides and grooms when terrible things happen. I say, this happens all the time. Don't worry. Although that's not true at all. This has never happened to me in my entire life. But what I tell her is I say, listen, Tina, these things do happen. I married Alicia, this amazing woman. But there were things I found out too after we got married. I had no idea that she liked Steely Dan, which honestly might have been a deal breaker if she had presented it to me before the wedding. I had no idea that my wife considers hampers like portable bureaus. Once clothes are folded in a hamper, they never have to move for the rest of eternity. They will just sit there forever unless I do something with them. That is the truth of our marriage. I had no idea that at night, when the last thing I want to do before I close my eyes is pull my wife close to me and hold her tight. I had no idea that the last thing my wife wants is for me to touch her. Get away from me. I don't sleep with people touching me, she tells me. And she goes to the other side of the bed. It's not me. It's just her. I tell Tina, I didn't know any of those things when I married my wife. And they're all terrible things about my wife. But I love her. None of that matters. And I tell her, this isn't going to matter either. And so we make a plan. I tell her, we're going to go back to the party. I'm going to make an excuse. I'm going to tell everyone you were at the gazebo enjoying the stars and now you're back to party. And you're going to go home tonight with Tim and in the morning over breakfast, you're going to tell Tim the truth that you're a smoker. And you're going to put on the doiest eyes. And you're going to quiver the lips, even if they're not quivering, you're going to pretend. You're going to give him that look that you can give him that will melt him and break his heart. And then together you'll make the plan on how you're going to quit smoking because he loves you. I have spent six hours with you and I know he loves you. And I know that this isn't going to come between you. That that's what marriage is about. That it's about finding the ugliness in each other and accepting it and when necessary, making it a little bit better. I don't know where Tim and Tina are today. That was about two years ago. I like to think they're together. I like to think Tina quit smoking. I like to think Tim supported her. I like to think that unlike my marriage, they get to sleep in each other's arms every night, loving and caring as opposed to the way I sleep, which is far on the other side of the bed with an enormous space between my wife and myself. But that physical space between us, it is the only space between us. Other than the sheets between us as we sleep, my wife and I are like this. We're as close as you can be. Despite her steely Dan, despite her hampers that work as bureaus. And despite the fact that she doesn't let me touch her when she sleeps at night. I love her more than anything. And I hope that Tina and Tim today feel the same way. Thanks everybody.
